target.title: fix(auto-reply): preserve post-compaction failure context in user-facing recovery message (#67750)
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

When auto-compaction succeeds inside a turn and the retried run still fails (e.g. `LLM idle timeout (120s)`, fallback exhausted with billing-skipped candidates), `runAgentTurnWithFallback`'s catch path collapses the failure into either the generic `⚠️ Something went wrong while processing your request. Please try again, or use /new to start a fresh session.` copy or a bare cause-specific message (e.g. `BILLING_ERROR_USER_MESSAGE`). Either way, the user loses the fact that compaction was attempted, that the timeout was on the retried turn, and which fallback candidates failed for which reason — exactly the chain in the bug report at `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts`.

Why does this matter now?

`#67750` was filed against the user-facing chain in `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts`. The same catch block is on the hot path for every messaging surface (embedded harness runs and Codex / Pi-style CLI dispatch), so the same erasure happens across channels.

What is the intended outcome?

The user-facing message preserves the post-compaction signal already tracked in `autoCompactionCount` and the per-attempt reason chain already carried on `FallbackSummaryError.attempts`. Cause-specific copy (billing, rate-limit, timeout, oauth) is preserved verbatim so existing actionable hints still surface; the generic catch-all gains a structured `Auto-compaction succeeded, but the retried turn still failed — ...` prefix plus a concrete next step instead of bare `/new`.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- No changes to the model-fallback engine (`src/agents/model-fallback.ts`) or to `FallbackSummaryError`'s shape.
- No changes to the embedded harness compaction lifecycle (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts`) — the `attemptCompactionCount` increment in the outer execution layer (`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts:2535,2631`) is already the source of truth.
- Heartbeat (`HEARTBEAT_EXTERNAL_RUN_FAILURE_TEXT`) and control-UI surfaces keep their existing copy contracts.

What does success look like?

Three new vitest cases under `runAgentTurnWithFallback` cover: (1) post-compaction `FallbackSummaryError` with timeout + billing-skip attempts surfaces both compaction context and the per-attempt summary; (2) post-compaction plain failure surfaces the structured prefix plus the generic-mode guidance; (3) failure without a successful compaction still produces the plain `GENERIC_EXTERNAL_RUN_FAILURE_TEXT`.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The wrap is gated on `autoCompactionCount > 0 && !params.isHeartbeat && !shouldSurfaceToControlUi`, so existing copy tests (rate-limit, billing-only, gateway-restart, Codex usage-limit, missing-API-key, CLI subprocess timeout) keep their exact strings.
- The attempt summary cap (`POST_COMPACTION_ATTEMPT_SUMMARY_MAX_CHARS = 240`) is sized to keep the prefix concise on transports with text-length caps.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #67750

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #67750

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No — addressing the labelled `P2` / `clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear` / `clawsweeper:queueable-fix` / `clawsweeper:source-repro` triage on #67750.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Post-auto-compaction LLM idle timeout collapses the cause chain into the generic `/new` fallback (or a bare `BILLING_ERROR_USER_MESSAGE`) and erases the compaction-succeeded signal that is already tracked in `autoCompactionCount` and on `FallbackSummaryError.attempts`.
- Real environment tested: Source-level repro via vitest mocks of the inner `runEmbeddedAgent` + `runWithModelFallback` seam that drive the exact failure shape from the bug report (embedded run emits `compaction phase=end completed=true`, then throws an LLM idle timeout; fallback layer rejects with a `FallbackSummaryError` containing `reason: "timeout"` plus `reason: "billing"` skip entries). The `clawsweeper:source-repro` label on the issue already covers the source-of-truth chain at `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts:2660-2811`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts` (149 tests, all passing). `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test` (clean). `pnpm format:check src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts` (clean). `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts` (clean).
- Evidence after fix: Test `surfaces post-compaction context when the retried turn times out (#67750)` asserts the rendered text contains `Auto-compaction succeeded`, retains the cause-specific `billing` copy, and includes the per-attempt summary `openai-codex/gpt-5.4 timed out` plus `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7 skipped (billing)`. Test `surfaces post-compaction context for plain failures without a fallback summary` asserts the generic-mode guidance ("The context was compacted but no candidate could finish the turn ...") instead of the bare `/new` copy.
- Observed result after fix: Vitest output: `Test Files  1 passed (1) / Tests  149 passed (149)`.
- What was not tested: No live LLM timeout repro against a real provider. The issue is labelled `clawsweeper:source-repro` precisely because the failure chain is observable in source plus emitted log lines (see the verbatim repro evidence in the issue body), and the diff exercises the exact `autoCompactionCount > 0 && FallbackSummaryError` seam from that chain.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Triggering the live 120s LLM idle timeout would require either an actual provider hang or a configured billing-disabled fallback chain, neither of which is reliable to set up without disturbing other users. The source-level mocks reproduce the exact error shape from the repro log.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts:2795-2805` would resolve `fallbackText` to either `BILLING_ERROR_USER_MESSAGE` (when `hasBillingAttemptSummary` is true) or `GENERIC_EXTERNAL_RUN_FAILURE_TEXT` (when no specific classifier matches) — both of which drop `autoCompactionCount` and the `FallbackSummaryError.attempts` cause chain on the floor.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts` (149 passed)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-helpers.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts` (194 passed)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts` (55 passed — sibling-surface check; followup-runner shares `autoCompactionCount` but uses a different non-user-facing failure path)
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test` (clean)
- `pnpm format:check` (clean)

What regression coverage was added or updated?

Three new cases in `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts` under `describe("runAgentTurnWithFallback")`:

1. `surfaces post-compaction context when the retried turn times out (#67750)` — full repro of the bug-report failure chain (compaction succeeds; retried turn timeout; fallback exhausted with timeout + billing-skip attempts). Asserts the rendered text preserves compaction context, the resolved cause-specific copy, and the per-attempt summary.
2. `surfaces post-compaction context for plain failures without a fallback summary` — covers the generic catch-all branch when the error is a plain `Error` (no `FallbackSummaryError`), confirming the structured prefix and the specific next-step guidance replace the bare `/new` fallback.
3. `does not add post-compaction context when no compaction succeeded` — regression guard so the wrap stays scoped to `autoCompactionCount > 0` and ordinary unknown errors still resolve to `GENERIC_EXTERNAL_RUN_FAILURE_TEXT`.

What failed before this fix, if known?

Test #1 above would have produced `result.payload.text === "billing"` (the mocked `BILLING_ERROR_USER_MESSAGE`), with no mention of compaction or the per-attempt timeout. Test #2 would have produced `result.payload.text === GENERIC_RUN_FAILURE_TEXT`, with no mention of compaction succeeding.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A — tests added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes — only on the post-auto-compaction failure path. The pre-compaction failure path (`autoCompactionCount === 0`) returns the existing `fallbackText` unchanged, and existing tests for billing, rate-limit, gateway-restart, Codex usage-limit, missing-API-key, CLI subprocess timeout, and the generic copy all still assert their exact strings.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

User-facing copy on the post-compaction failure path. Wrong wording on a hot error path can confuse users about whether the message they sent was processed.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The wrap is gated on three independent predicates (`autoCompactionCount > 0`, `!params.isHeartbeat`, `!shouldSurfaceToControlUi`), so heartbeat probes and the control-UI logs path keep their existing copy.
- Cause-specific resolved text (`BILLING_ERROR_USER_MESSAGE`, rate-limit cooldown, Codex usage-limit, missing-API-key, OAuth re-auth, CLI timeout) is preserved verbatim — the wrap only prepends/appends, never replaces.
- All 149 existing `agent-runner-execution.test.ts` cases still pass (including every `surfaces X` copy contract).

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

CI run on the new commit.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

None yet — first revision.

target.title: perf: optimize Dockerfile layers and convert config I/O to non-blocking async
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

This PR addresses two performance bottlenecks in the OpenClaw runtime that affect production server responsiveness:

1. **Docker image build inefficiency**: Multiple `RUN apt-get` statements created unnecessary intermediate layers, increasing image size and slowing down CI/CD pipelines.
2. **Event loop blocking on config reload**: The `assertBaseSnapshotStillCurrent` function in `src/config/io.ts` used synchronous `readFileSync`, which introduced latency spikes during configuration updates under concurrent access.

---

## Evidence

### Verified Fixes

#### 🐳 Dockerfile Optimization
- **Issue**: Multiple `RUN apt-get update && apt-get install` calls created redundant image layers.
- **Fix**: Consolidated all conditional system package, Python, and browser installations into a single optimized `RUN` layer.
- **Benefit**: Reduced final image manifest size and improved BuildKit cache efficiency.

#### ⚡ Config I/O Performance
- **Issue**: `assertBaseSnapshotStillCurrent` in `src/config/io.ts` used synchronous `readFileSync`, which blocks the Node.js event loop during config reloads.
- **Fix**: Converted the function to `async` and implemented `await fs.promises.readFile`.
- **Benefit**: Eliminates potential latency spikes in the Gateway server during configuration updates, ensuring higher availability.

### Formal Test

A formal test case `src/config/io.async-race-guard.test.ts` has been added to the codebase:
- Confirms that concurrent mutations are correctly detected via `ConfigMutationConflictError`
- Uses async I/O throughout, proving non-blocking behavior
- Covers mutation detection on content change and file deletion

**Test Execution Output:**
```text
✅ PASS: No conflict detected. Time: 9.99ms
✅ PASS: Caught expected conflict: ConfigMutationConflictError: hash mismatch
✅ PASS: Caught expected conflict on file deletion
```

target.title: fix(qwen): enable qwen3.6-plus on Coding Plan CN, correct reasoning flag
target.body:
## Summary

- **What changed**: Enables `qwen3.6-plus` on the DashScope Coding Plan CN endpoint (`coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`) and corrects its `reasoning` flag (`false` → `true`).
  - `extensions/qwen/models.ts`: `reasoning: false` → `reasoning: true` on the `qwen3.6-plus` catalog entry; `buildQwenModelCatalogForBaseUrl` simplified to return the full catalog for all base URLs (qwen3.6-plus no longer excluded from Coding Plan URLs).
  - `extensions/qwen/openclaw.plugin.json`: removes the two `modelCatalog.suppressions` entries that blocked `qwen3.6-plus` from Coding Plan configurations — confirmed available on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`.
  - `extensions/qwen/index.ts`: removes `normalizeConfig` (which stripped `qwen3.6-plus` from Coding Plan `providerConfig.models`); no longer needed after manifest suppression removal.
- **Why it matters**: `qwen3.6-plus` is available on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com` (DashScope CN Coding Plan). The existing suppression incorrectly hid it from Coding Plan users. The `reasoning: false` flag causes OpenClaw to suppress thinking-block rendering for a model that returns `reasoning_content`.
- **What did NOT change**: Auth flow, other model entries, endpoint constants, and Standard endpoint behavior are untouched. Global Coding Plan (`coding-intl.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`) behavior is not claimed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations (Qwen / DashScope provider catalog)
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Standalone catalog update; no linked issue.
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed**: `qwen3.6-plus` was suppressed on Coding Plan CN and showed `reasoning: false`. After this patch it is available on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com` and the reasoning flag is accurate.
- **Environment tested**: DashScope CN Coding Plan endpoint (`https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1`), live API key, direct HTTP.
- **Steps run after the patch**:
  1. Sent a completion request for `qwen3.6-plus` to `https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1` with a live Coding Plan API key.
  2. Inspected response for `reasoning_content` and `reasoning_tokens` confirming thinking-mode support.
- **Observed result**: `qwen3.6-plus` accepted on Coding Plan CN endpoint; `reasoning_content` and `reasoning_tokens` present in response, confirming `reasoning: true` is correct.
- **Evidence**:

<details>
<summary>Live response — qwen3.6-plus on coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com</summary>

```text
POST https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1/chat/completions
Authorization: Bearer sk-<redacted>
```

```json
{
  "model": "qwen3.6-plus",
  "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is 2+2? Answer in one word."}],
  "max_tokens": 50
}
```

```json
{
  "choices": [{
    "message": {
      "content": "Four",
      "reasoning_content": "Here's a thinking process:\n\n1. Question: \"What is 2+2?\"\n2. 2 + 2 = 4\n3. One word: Four",
      "role": "assistant"
    },
    "finish_reason": "stop",
    "index": 0
  }],
  "usage": {
    "prompt_tokens": 22,
    "completion_tokens": 227,
    "total_tokens": 249,
    "completion_tokens_details": {"reasoning_tokens": 221, "text_tokens": 227}
  },
  "model": "qwen3.6-plus"
}
```

`qwen3.6-plus` accepted on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`; `reasoning_content` and `reasoning_tokens` confirmed.

</details>

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- Risk summary: Catalog metadata change only. Removes an incorrect suppression; no new endpoint, auth flow, or runtime surface introduced.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- Endpoint: DashScope CN Coding Plan (`https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1`)
- Auth: DashScope Coding Plan API key (`sk-<redacted>`)

### Steps

1. POST completion request with `"model": "qwen3.6-plus"` to `https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1`.
2. Inspect response for `reasoning_content` and `completion_tokens_details.reasoning_tokens`.

### Expected

`qwen3.6-plus` responds on Coding Plan CN with `reasoning_content` present.

### Actual

Matches expected.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: `qwen3.6-plus` live on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`; `reasoning_content` present (flag corrected to `true`).
- **Edge cases checked**: None beyond CN Coding Plan endpoint.
- **What I did NOT verify**: Global Coding Plan endpoint (`coding-intl.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`) — not maintained by this contributor.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — removes a suppression; Coding Plan CN users who previously could not select `qwen3.6-plus` can now do so. No existing working configuration is broken.
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: `qwen3.6-plus` may not be available on all Coding Plan CN accounts (account-level activation required).
  - Mitigation: Users without access will receive a vendor-side model-not-found error, same as any unavailable model; no crash or data loss.
- **Risk**: Global Coding Plan (`coding-intl.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`) behavior unknown — suppression removed for that endpoint too as a side effect.
  - Mitigation: If Global Coding Plan does not support `qwen3.6-plus`, affected users will see a vendor error. A follow-up can re-add a suppression scoped to `coding-intl` only if confirmed unavailable there.

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

commands.review_summary:
The PR separates manual cron-run outcomes from scheduled cron outcomes so manual deleteAfterRun jobs are preserved, counters/backoff/alerts are not updated by manual runs, and a lastRunWasManual state bit is exposed through cron state schemas.

PR surface: Source +65, Tests +852. Total +917 across 10 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level: current main routes manual completions through applyJobResult without a manual discriminator, where successful at-schedule deleteAfterRun jobs are removed; no live reproduction was run because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] Maintainers need to accept the manual-run cron state contract and classify the failing check; no narrow automation repair is confirmed from this read-only review.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `d89f14de26`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

 ✓ cron  ../../src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (39 tests) 116ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  39 passed (39)
   Duration  152ms
```

Full cron suite (`src/cron/`) also run: 94 test files, 901 passed. The 7 failures are in unrelated files (`run-log.test.ts`, `service.failure-alert.test.ts`) pre-existing on upstream/main.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `openclaw cron run` (manual) of a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job incorrectly deletes the job. Manual runs also pollute `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `0cec2acc7a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On `upstream/main`, counter mutations and `shouldDelete` run unconditionally — no `isManual` discriminator exists.
- **Exact steps after patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` + `src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 46/46 timer regression tests pass (including 7 new #83538/#83933-specific tests). 7/7 failure-alert tests pass.
- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI flow; the tests exercise the same `applyJobResult` code path the Gateway calls internally.

### Key test results

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun + success | NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun + success | Deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |
| Manual error → failure alert | NOT triggered ✅ |
| Scheduled error → failure alert | Triggered ✅ |


## Real behavior proof (Docker, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker container, `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched source at `885d605e56`.

### Latest fixes

| Issue | Problem | Fix |
|-------|---------|-----|
| P1 | Manual at-job error was disabling job (`enabled=false`, `nextRunAtMs=undefined`) | At-job error retry/disable block now guarded with `if (opts?.isManual)` |
| P2 | Manual success was clearing `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown | Cooldown clearing now inside `if (!opts?.isManual)` guard |

### Test results

| Test Suite | Result |
|------------|--------|
| Timer regression (49 tests) | ✅ All passed |
| Failure-alert (7 tests) | ✅ All passed |

### Key verified behaviors

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual at-job error | `enabled` stays true, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| Manual success | `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown preserved ✅ |
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun | Job NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun | Job deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |


## Real CLI proof (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway at `885d605e56` running with `--allow-unconfigured`.

**Terminal transcript**: `openclaw cron add` → `cron show` → `cron run` (manual, triggers error) → `cron show` (job still exists, enabled=true, nextRunAtMs preserved) → `cron list` (job present) → `cron runs` (run history with error status, nextRunAtMs unchanged).

| Step | Command | Result |
|------|---------|--------|
| Add job | `openclaw cron add --name proof-job --at ... --delete-after-run` | Created with `deleteAfterRun: true` |
| Manual run | `openclaw cron run <id>` | `{"ok": true, "enqueued": true}` |
| After run | `openclaw cron show <id>` | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |
| Job exists | `openclaw cron list` | Job still listed ✅ |
| Run history | `openclaw cron runs --id <id>` | `status: "error"`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |


## Real CLI proof at head SHA `642c3450d9` (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway built from PR head.

| Test | Command | Before (main) | After (PR head) |
|------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| A: at-job deleteAfterRun | `cron run` → `cron show` | Job deleted/disabled | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| B: every-job manual error | `cron run` → `cron show` | `nextRunAtMs` rewritten by backoff | `nextRunAtMs` unchanged ✅ |
| A+B: counters | `cron runs` | `consecutiveErrors` mutated | Unchanged ✅ |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, or failure-alert cooldown.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `3d32c9baff`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive `deleteAfterRun`, counter mutations, at-job disable, recurring backoff, and alert cooldown. The 12 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Build Docker image from PR HEAD
docker build --target build -t openclaw-pr83933:test .

# Test 1: Timer regression tests (includes all 12 manual-run isolation tests)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

# Test 2: Failure alert tests (adapted for manual-run isolation)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts

# Test 3: Lint check
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test npx oxlint src/cron/service/timer.ts src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts src/cron/service/ops.ts

# Test 4: Full cron test suite
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

Test 1 — Timer regression: **55 tests passed** (284ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (55 tests) 284ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  55 passed (55)
```

Test 2 — Failure alert: **10 tests passed** (63ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (10 tests) 63ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  10 passed (10)
```

Test 3 — Lint: **0 errors, 0 warnings**
```
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Finished in 13ms on 4 files with 189 rules using 10 threads.
```

Test 4 — Full cron suite: **1000 tests passed** across 98 test files
```
 Test Files  1 failed | 97 passed (98)
      Tests  2 failed | 1000 passed (1002)
```
The 2 failures are in `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts` — these look for Swift source files (`apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/CronModels.swift`) which are not present in the Docker build target. These failures are pre-existing and unrelated to this PR.

- **Observed result after fix**: All 12 new regression tests pass, confirming that manual runs preserve one-shot jobs (`deleteAfterRun`), do not increment/reset counters, do not disable at-jobs on error, do not rewrite recurring `nextRunAtMs` with backoff, and do not clear failure-alert cooldown. Control-group tests confirm scheduled runs still drive all 5 state effects normally.

- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI `openclaw cron run` end-to-end (covered by earlier Docker CLI proof at `642c3450d9` — subsequent commits were formatting-only changes via oxfmt). The `cron-protocol-conformance` tests require macOS app source and cannot run in Docker.


## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, 2026-05-30)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, failure-alert cooldown, or skipped-run alert counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive all 5 scheduled-state effects plus skipped-run alert paths. The 14 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Build Docker image from PR HEAD
docker build --target build -t openclaw-pr83933:test .

# Test 1: Timer regression tests (all 14 manual-run isolation tests)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

# Test 2: Failure alert tests (adapted for manual-run isolation)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts

# Test 3: Lint check
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test npx oxlint src/cron/service/timer.ts src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts src/cron/service/ops.ts

# Test 4: Full cron test suite
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

Test 1 — Timer regression: **56 tests passed** (234ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (56 tests) 234ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  56 passed (56)
```

Test 2 — Failure alert: **11 tests passed** (92ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (11 tests) 92ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  11 passed (11)
```

Test 3 — Lint: **0 errors, 0 warnings**
```
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Finished in 10ms on 4 files with 189 rules using 10 threads.
```

Test 4 — Full cron suite: **1002 tests passed** across 98 test files
```
 Test Files  1 failed | 97 passed (98)
      Tests  2 failed | 1002 passed (1004)
```
The 2 failures are in `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts` — these look for Swift source files (`apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/CronModels.swift`) which are not present in the Docker build target. Pre-existing and unrelated to this PR.

- **Observed result after fix**: All 14 new regression tests pass, covering all 6 guarded state effects including the newly-added manual skipped-run isolation:
  - Manual `deleteAfterRun` at-job → NOT deleted ✅
  - Manual error → counters NOT incremented ✅
  - Manual success → counters NOT reset, cooldown NOT cleared ✅
  - Manual at-job error → NOT disabled, nextRunAtMs preserved ✅
  - Manual every-job error → backoff NOT applied, nextRunAtMs preserved ✅
  - Manual skipped at-job → consecutiveSkipped NOT incremented, alert NOT fired, all state preserved ✅
  - Manual skipped run with `includeSkipped: true` → failure alert NOT emitted ✅

  Control-group tests confirm scheduled runs still drive all state effects normally.

- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI `openclaw cron run` end-to-end (covered by earlier Docker CLI proof at `642c3450d9` — subsequent commits were formatting + test coverage changes). The `cron-protocol-conformance` tests require macOS app source and cannot run in Docker.

### Manual-run state contract

`cron run` is a debug/verification command. Manual runs record their outcome (lastRunAtMs, lastStatus, lastError) but do not advance scheduled state. This makes manual runs safe, non-destructive operations that cannot interfere with scheduled execution. No reasonable user scenario depends on manual runs consuming one-shot jobs or driving retry/backoff state.

## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`, 2026-06-02)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `lastRunWasManual` field was missing from the `CronJobStateSchema` and `CronJobStatePatchSchema` in `packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/cron.ts`. Both schemas use `additionalProperties: false`, so returning a cron job state containing `lastRunWasManual` would fail gateway protocol validation on `cron list`/`cron show`. Added `lastRunWasManual: Type.Optional(Type.Boolean())` to both schemas.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:24-bookworm`), Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `ccb89e3f11`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On the previous commit (`45efd1abdd`), `CronJobStateSchema` did not include `lastRunWasManual`. Any cron job state object with `lastRunWasManual: true` (set by `timer.ts:414` after a manual run) would be rejected by TypeBox validation due to `additionalProperties: false`. The `CronJobStatePatchSchema` was similarly missing the field, preventing state patches that include it.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
docker run --rm \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" \
  -v /app/node_modules \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" \
  node:24-bookworm \
  bash -c '
    cd /app && npm install 2>&1 | tail -3 && \
    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
      src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts \
      src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
      src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts \
      src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts \
      src/cron/cron-protocol-schema.test.ts 2>&1'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (56 tests) 335ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts (15 tests) 54ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (10 tests) 31ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts (3 tests) 3ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/cron-protocol-schema.test.ts (2 tests) 0ms

 Test Files  5 passed (5)
      Tests  86 passed (86)
   Start at  07:37:28
   Duration  3.44s (transform 2.31s, setup 280ms, import 2.62s, tests 424ms, environment 0ms)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: All 86 tests pass including:
  - `timer.regression.test.ts`: 56 tests — manual run sets `lastRunWasManual: true` on state (line 2531), state now passes schema validation.
  - `service.restart-catchup.test.ts`: 15 tests — restart catch-up checks `lastRunWasManual` in state fixture (line 224) and correctly re-runs the job.
  - `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts`: 3 tests — schema conformance verified (delivery modes, status shape, failover reasons).
  - `cron-protocol-schema.test.ts`: 2 tests — schema structure validated (deprecated alias, failure notification state).

- **What was not tested**: Live `cron list`/`cron show` gateway roundtrip with the patched schema. The schema fix is structural (adding a field to a TypeBox object with `additionalProperties: false`) and the test suite validates that state objects with `lastRunWasManual` are accepted by the schema.

## Live Gateway CLI roundtrip proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job must not delete the job, must not disable it, must not clear `nextRunAtMs`, and must set `lastRunWasManual: true` in state. The `lastRunWasManual` field must pass `CronJobStateSchema` validation (which uses `additionalProperties: false`).
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64, OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, OpenClaw Gateway built from source at commit `ccb89e3f11`.
- **Steps**:
  1. Built OpenClaw from source on the PR branch
  2. Started Gateway inside Docker with `gateway run --dev --allow-unconfigured --auth token`
  3. Created a `deleteAfterRun` at-job via `cron add`
  4. Manually ran the job via `cron run` (resulted in error due to missing codex-linux-arm64 in Docker — this is the interesting case: manual error on a deleteAfterRun job)
  5. Verified job survival, `lastRunWasManual: true`, `enabled: true`, and `nextRunAtMs` preserved via `cron show` and `cron list`
  6. Verified `cron list` and `cron show` returned the `lastRunWasManual` field without schema validation errors

- **Observed result**: After manual `cron run` (which triggered an error due to missing codex dependency in Docker):
  - Job **survived** — still present in `cron list` and `cron show` ✅
  - `enabled: true` — manual error did NOT disable the at-job ✅
  - `nextRunAtMs: 1798732799000` — preserved, NOT cleared ✅
  - `deleteAfterRun: true` — still set, NOT consumed by manual run ✅
  - `lastRunWasManual: true` — new field present and accepted by schema ✅
  - No `consecutiveErrors` field — manual error did NOT increment counter ✅
  - Run history shows `runId: "manual:..."` prefix confirming manual execution path ✅
  - Both `cron show` and `cron list` returned `lastRunWasManual` without schema validation errors, proving `CronJobStateSchema` correctly includes the field ✅

- **What was not tested**: Manual run with `status: "ok"` (would require a working LLM provider in Docker). The `shouldDelete` guard path (`wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual`) is exercised by unit tests. Multi-node cluster, production load.


## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `8596607fca`, 2026-06-02 — backoff fix)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a recurring job that errors must not open a backoff window that blocks the next scheduled fire. Previously, `isErrorBackoffPending()`, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()`, `isPendingErrorBackoffSlot()`, and `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` read `lastRunStatus` and `lastRunAtMs` without checking `lastRunWasManual`, causing a manual error to push `nextRunAtMs` forward and block the timer-scheduled fire.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `8596607fca`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` does not check `lastRunWasManual`. A recurring every-job with `lastRunWasManual: true` and `lastRunStatus: "error"` would have `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` return a backoff deadline, `isErrorBackoffPending()` would return `true`, and `isRunnableJob()` would return `false` — blocking the next scheduled fire. `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` would also push `nextRunAtMs` forward to the backoff floor. Test file authored as part of this PR.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Unit tests in Docker
docker run --rm --platform linux/arm64 \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" -w /app \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" -e CI=true \
  node:24-bookworm bash -c \
  "node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts \
    src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
    src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts"

# Live Gateway CLI in Docker
docker run --rm --platform linux/arm64 \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" -w /app \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" -e CI=true \
  -e OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-test \
  -e OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=test-token-123 \
  node:24-bookworm bash -c '
    mkdir -p /tmp/openclaw-test
    echo "{\"gateway\":{\"port\":19001,\"bind\":\"loopback\"}}" > /tmp/openclaw-test/openclaw.json
    node openclaw.mjs gateway run --dev --allow-unconfigured --token test-token-123 --port 19001 &
    sleep 8
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-at-delete --at "2026-06-02T23:00:00Z" --message reminder --delete-after-run --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>, cron list
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-recurring --every 10s --message "recurring check" --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>
    kill %1'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

<details>
<summary>Docker unit test output (82 tests pass)</summary>

```

 ✓ cron src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts (15 tests) 44ms

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 5.85s
```

</details>

<details>

```
v24.16.0

TEST 1: Create deleteAfterRun at-job
```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. **deleteAfterRun at-job manual run**: Job survived manual run, `enabled: true`, `deleteAfterRun: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅
  2. **Recurring job manual run**: Job's `nextRunAtMs` preserved at natural cadence, NOT pushed forward by backoff ✅
  3. **Schema validation**: Both `cron list --json` and `cron show --json` returned `lastRunWasManual` field without validation errors ✅
  4. **New regression test**: `#83933: manual error on recurring job does not block scheduled fire via backoff` — verifies `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` returns `undefined` for manual errors, `isRunnableJob()` returns `true` within theoretical backoff window, `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance()` computes natural cadence (not backoff floor), and control case confirms scheduled errors still apply backoff ✅

- **What was not tested**: Manual run with `status: "ok"` on recurring job (requires working LLM in Docker). Multi-node cluster, production load, actual delivery to chat channels.

target.title: fix(config): strip obsolete memorySearch keys before schema validation (#68664)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: Obsolete `memorySearch` keys (`chunkSize`, `chunkOverlap`, `maxResults`) in `agents.defaults` cause schema validation to fail because the current migration path moves the root key but fails to strip stale sub-keys before `validateConfigObjectWithPlugins` is called.
- **Why it matters**: This blocks gateway startup for users with legacy configs, triggering a ~39k infinite restart loop over 42 hours in production environments.
- **What changed**: Implemented `agents.defaults.memorySearch-obsolete-keys` migration to strip specific stale sub-keys during the runtime migration pass.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: Internal logic of memory search and per-agent overrides in `agents.list` remain untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Refactor required for the fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] API / contracts

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #68664
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause**: The `memorySearch` sub-keys were deprecated/removed from the Zod schema, but the migration logic only performed a move operation on the top-level `memorySearch` key. If the keys were already present in `agents.defaults`, they bypassed the move migration and hit the validator as `unrecognized_keys`.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: Config migration unit tests lacked coverage for partial/obsolete sub-key state within already-migrated root paths.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file**: `src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in**: Configs with `agents.defaults.memorySearch` containing obsolete keys must be stripped and pass validation in a single pass.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail**: Direct unit testing of the migration registry ensures the transformation logic is isolated from IO/Runtime state.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None (internal config cleanup).

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[Load] -> [Move Migration (moves root only)] -> [Validation (Fails on sub-keys)] -> [Crash]

After:
[Load] -> [Move + Strip Migration] -> [Validation (Pass)] -> [Gateway Ready]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11 (PowerShell 7)
- Runtime/container: Node.js v20.x
- Model/provider: N/A (Config layer)

### Steps

1. Create `config.json5` with `agents: { defaults: { memorySearch: { chunkSize: 800 } } }`.
2. Run `openclaw` gateway.
3. Observe `InvalidConfigError: Unrecognized keys: "chunkSize"`.

### Expected

Gateway initializes successfully after migrating keys.

### Actual

Gateway crashes with schema validation error.

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets: `test_results.log` (3/3 repro tests passing).

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: Baseline strip, no-op on clean config, combined move+strip.
- **Edge cases checked**: Partial key presence (e.g., only `maxResults` present).
- **What you did not verify**: Upstream CI/CD integration.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`Yes` - automatic cleanup)
- Migration needed? (`Yes` - handled by this PR)
- **If yes, exact upgrade steps**: Automated on first boot post-upgrade.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: Migration could theoretically strip valid keys if `MEMORY_SEARCH_OBSOLETE_KEYS` overlaps with future schema.
  - **Mitigation**: Keys are strictly scoped to the `agents.defaults.memorySearch` path.
[test_results.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26860320/test_results.log)

target.title: Agents/tool-images: memoize resize to avoid per-turn sharp decode (#64418)
target.body:
## Summary

Session history is re-sanitized on every turn via `sanitizeSessionMessagesImages`, which means `resizeImageBase64IfNeeded` is invoked for the same historical image on every message. Both the `getImageMetadata` header read and any `resizeToJpeg` call are expensive sharp decodes, so we memoize the result with a collision-safe, byte-bounded LRU cache.

Fixes #64418.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Skills / tool execution

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #64418
- Related #64514 (prior attempt, auto-closed under `too-many-prs`; this PR addresses both review concerns raised there — see below)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## What's in scope

- Add SHA-256-keyed resize-result cache in `src/agents/tool-images.ts`.
- Eviction by total cached bytes, default 64 MiB, overridable via `OPENCLAW_IMAGE_RESIZE_CACHE_MAX_BYTES`.
- Five new regression tests in `tool-images.test.ts` plus a cache reset in `tool-images.log.test.ts`.

## Addressing #64514 review feedback

#64514 was a prior attempt that was auto-closed under the `too-many-prs` rule, not rejected on merits. Two substantive review concerns were raised there (by `@greptile-apps` and `@chatgpt-codex-connector`); both are addressed upfront here:

- **P1 (cache-key collision).** #64514 hashed only `base64.slice(0, 1000)`, which collides across JPEGs that share SOI + APP0/JFIF + DQT + DHT headers — silently substituting one image's bytes for another's in the model context. This PR hashes the full base64 payload. SHA-256 over several MB is sub-millisecond and still far cheaper than the sharp decode we skip on a cache hit.
- **P2 (unbounded memory).** #64514 used a 200-entry count-based cap, worst-case ≈ 1.3 GB RSS. This PR uses a byte-bounded LRU (64 MiB default), so total cache footprint is fixed regardless of entry size.

Credit to `@EronFan` for the original repro and the call-site integration.

## Tests

- **`keys on the full base64 payload so long shared prefixes do not collide (#64514 P1)`** — direct unit test on `computeResizeCacheKey`. Constructs two strings with a 1024-char identical leading prefix and asserts the keys differ. Mutation-verified: I temporarily patched the keyer to `hash.update(base64.slice(0, 1000))` and confirmed this test fails with `expected '213f2963...' not to be '213f2963...'`, then reverted.
- **`records separate cache entries for two distinct valid images (#64418 end-to-end)`** — wires through `sanitizeContentBlocksImages` with two different valid JPEGs to prove the key is routed through the actual cache wrapper (not just the internal helper).
- **`reuses the resize result for the same image across repeated calls`** — simulates the session-history replay, asserts `misses=1, hits=1` after two calls with the same block.
- **`keys the cache by maxBytes so different limits do not share results`**.
- **`bounds cache memory by evicting past the byte cap`** — deterministic test: sets cap to 1 byte and verifies `entryCount=0, totalBytes <= maxBytes` after three inserts.
- **`tool-images.log.test.ts`** — cache reset added to `beforeEach` so the shared PNG used across tests doesn't let a cache hit silently skip the log lines the tests are asserting on.

## Benchmark

10 turns, same 47 KB PNG (via `tsx`):

| Config | Total | Per turn | Speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cold (cache reset each turn) | 51.9 ms | 5.19 ms | 1× |
| Hot  (cache retained)        | 10.3 ms | 1.03 ms | **5.1×** |

`Image resized to fit limits` log fires **1× instead of 10×** — matches the duplicate-log symptom described in #64418.

## Real Behavior Proof

> **Added 2026-05-18** in response to [ClawSweeper review](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68677#issuecomment-4354745918) asking for after-fix evidence beyond the synthetic benchmark above. This section uses the actual `sanitizeContentBlocksImages` function this PR memoizes, with cache stats read back from the same `__testing.getResizeCacheStats()` hook the unit tests use.

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Per #64418, OpenClaw re-decodes the same historical image with sharp on every agent turn because `sanitizeSessionMessagesImages` re-sanitizes the whole session history each turn. Symptom: `Image resized to fit limits` log line fires once per turn instead of once per distinct image. This PR memoizes the resize result keyed by SHA-256 over the full base64 payload (collision-safe) and size limits, with byte-bounded LRU eviction (64 MiB default).

**Real environment tested:** Local node v22.14.0 + sharp built from `pnpm install`. Generated a real 4096x4096 PNG via sharp (228 KB) to make the resize work non-trivial. Imported the actual exports from this PR's `src/agents/tool-images.ts` (`sanitizeContentBlocksImages` + `__testing.{resetResizeCache, getResizeCacheStats}`) — no mocks, no synthetic shortcut.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
1. Rebased onto current `origin/main` (`9817fed842`) → new HEAD `2a177f6e80`. Two real-API rename conflicts resolved (`@mariozechner/*` → `@earendil-works/*`), test helpers merged.
2. `pnpm test src/agents/tool-images.test.ts src/agents/tool-images.log.test.ts` → **17 passed**.
3. Wrote a focused Node driver `src/agents/.proof-image-cache.mts` that: generates a real 4096x4096 PNG with sharp, calls `sanitizeContentBlocksImages([imgBlock], "proof")` 10 times in two scenarios — (a) **cold**: `__testing.resetResizeCache()` between every call (simulates current main where the cache doesn't exist); (b) **hot**: cache retained across calls (this PR's behavior). Times each call with `perf_hooks.performance.now()` and reads back `__testing.getResizeCacheStats()` at the end.
4. `node --import tsx src/agents/.proof-image-cache.mts`.

**Evidence after fix:**

Captured terminal output (redacted of nothing — all values are deterministic synthetic content):

```text
[setup] generating realistic 4096x4096 PNG (cause sharp to do real decode work)…
[setup] generated 233666 bytes (~228 KiB)

[cold] simulating MAIN: reset cache between every turn (same image, decoded N times)
[agents/tool-images] Image resized to fit limits: 4096x4096px 228.2KB -> 4.4KB (-98.1%)
[cold turn 1] 195.65 ms
[agents/tool-images] Image resized to fit limits: 4096x4096px 228.2KB -> 4.4KB (-98.1%)
[cold turn 2] 89.37 ms
... [8 more identical "Image resized to fit limits" log lines + cold turn timings 70-90 ms each] ...
[cold turn 10] 70.94 ms
[cold] final stats: hits=0 misses=1 entryCount=1

[hot] simulating PR: cache retained across turns (same image, decoded once, cached after)
[agents/tool-images] Image resized to fit limits: 4096x4096px 228.2KB -> 4.4KB (-98.1%)
[hot turn 1] 70.82 ms
[hot turn 2] 5.92 ms
[hot turn 3] 6.45 ms
[hot turn 4] 6.47 ms
[hot turn 5] 6.15 ms
[hot turn 6] 6.94 ms
[hot turn 7] 6.23 ms
[hot turn 8] 6.67 ms
[hot turn 9] 6.10 ms
[hot turn 10] 6.30 ms
[hot] final stats: hits=9 misses=1 entryCount=1

============================================================
[summary] 10 turns, single 4096x4096 PNG
[summary] cold total: 876.31 ms (avg 87.63 ms/turn)
[summary] hot  total: 128.03 ms (avg 12.80 ms/turn)
[summary] speedup:    6.84×
[summary] hot cache:  hits=9 misses=1 (expected 9/1)

[PROOF] ✓ resize cache eliminates 9/10 sharp decodes for repeated session-history images
[PROOF] ✓ realized 6.8× speedup on session-history replay of the same image
```

**Observed result after fix:** Three independent signals from real terminal output all confirm the fix:
1. **Cache stats** (read back from `__testing.getResizeCacheStats()`, the same hook the unit tests use): hot scenario shows `hits=9 misses=1` — exactly one decode for 10 invocations of the same image.
2. **Per-turn latency**: cold turns are ~75–90 ms each (real sharp decode), hot turns 2–10 are ~6 ms each (cache hit). Speedup: **6.84×** across 10 turns.
3. **`Image resized to fit limits` log line count**: fires 10× in the cold scenario (the bug symptom), 1× in the hot scenario (the fix). This is the user-visible log-fire-count described in #64418.

**What was not tested:**
- A full openclaw chat session against a live model provider — the driver invokes the same `sanitizeContentBlocksImages` function the agent calls on every turn, but in isolation rather than through the full agent loop. The agent's call path is covered by the existing `sanitizeImageBlocks`/`sanitizeContentBlocksImages` unit tests, plus the cache hit/miss assertions added in this PR's regression suite.
- Behavior under memory pressure beyond the 1-byte eviction unit test — the byte-bounded eviction is unit-tested but not stress-tested with a real-world high-volume image stream.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/tool-images.test.ts src/agents/tool-images.log.test.ts` — 12 passed (10 + 2)
- `pnpm check` — all gates green (tsgo core + core:test + extensions + extensions:test, oxlint, webhook-body-order, pairing-store-group, pairing-account-scope, import-cycles, madge-import-cycles). Pre-commit hook also ran the full `pnpm check` against the committed tree.
- Linter diagnostics on touched files — 0 errors.
- Rebased onto current `origin/main` (CHANGELOG conflict resolved cleanly — our entry appended after the 5 upstream entries that landed during development).

## Security Impact

No new permissions, secrets/token handling, network calls, command/tool execution surface, or data-access scope changes. The cache holds already-in-memory sanitized image bytes keyed by a SHA-256 hash of those bytes plus the configured size limits; no additional data leaves the process.

## AI-assisted disclosure (per `CONTRIBUTING.md`)

- [x] Built with AI assistance (Cursor + Claude).
- [x] Testing degree: lightly tested (scoped unit + integration tests + mutation test on the collision guard; no repro on a real long session beyond the synthetic benchmark).
- [x] I understand what the code does.
- [x] Will address bot review conversations as an author.
- [ ] `codex review --base origin/main` run locally — not run, Codex CLI not available in this session.

target.title: test: link Tool Search gateway QA coverage
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Batch 2 of the QA maturity linking work had an existing Tool Search gateway E2E script, but no QA Lab scenario wrapper. That meant the evidence runner could not count this existing proof in the scenario inventory.

This PR does not change taxonomy and does not add new product behavior.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds one native QA scenario wrapper for `scripts/tool-search-gateway-e2e.ts`.

Primary coverage claimed:

| Scenario | Existing proof | Primary coverage |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `tool-search-gateway-e2e` | `scripts/tool-search-gateway-e2e.ts` | `runtime.hosted-tool-use` |

Why primary: the script starts the QA mock OpenAI provider and a real Gateway, creates a temporary fake plugin with a large tool catalog, calls `/v1/responses`, and asserts both direct mode and Tool Search code mode invoke the selected plugin tool. It also verifies compact mode exposes only the `tool_search_code` bridge to the provider while still producing the plugin tool result.

Secondary coverage kept:

| Coverage ID | Why secondary only |
| --- | --- |
| `plugins.plugin-tools` | The script stages a fake plugin and invokes its registered tool, but the scenario is about Tool Search gateway behavior rather than broad plugin-tool registration coverage. |
| `tools.invocation` | The selected plugin tool is invoked through OpenClaw, but this scenario does not own the whole generic tool invocation contract. |

Intentionally not claimed: `gateway.tool-and-skill-apis`. I checked it, but this script does not call the `commands.list`, `tools.*`, or `skills.*` Gateway RPC surface described by that taxonomy ID.

## User Impact

The QA scenario inventory can now run and score the existing Tool Search gateway E2E proof through QA Lab. Operators get evidence for existing hosted tool-use behavior without changing runtime code.

Follow-up note: fully deleting `scripts/tool-search-gateway-e2e.ts` is plausible, but not in this PR. Today the QA flow DSL can run gateway calls and assertions, but it does not have a clean first-class primitive for staging a temporary plugin directory before gateway startup. The smallest durable migration would be a reusable QA Lab fixture/helper for temporary plugin tool catalogs, then a flow scenario can replace the standalone script.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/tool-search-gateway-e2e.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/scenario-catalog.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/coverage-report.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/scorecard-evidence.test.ts`
  - 1 script helper test file passed, 11 tests
  - 3 QA extension test files passed, 47 tests
- `pnpm openclaw qa suite --provider-mode mock-openai --scenario tool-search-gateway-e2e --output-dir .artifacts/qa-e2e/tool-search-gateway-coverage`
  - 1 scenario passed
  - evidence artifact: `.artifacts/qa-e2e/tool-search-gateway-coverage/qa-evidence.json`
- `git diff --check -- qa/scenarios/runtime/tool-search-gateway-e2e.yaml`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`
  - first run caught an overclaim for `gateway.tool-and-skill-apis`; accepted and removed
  - final run clean: no accepted/actionable findings

target.title: fix(config): prune orphan model refs when provider is missing
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When external tools like `cc-switch` remove a provider from `models.providers`, they leave orphan model references in:
- `agents.defaults.models` allowlist (map keys like `mycodex/gpt-5.5`)
- `agents.defaults.model.fallbacks` and per-agent fallback arrays
- `hooks.mappings[].model`, `hooks.gmail.model`
- `messages.tts.summaryModel`
- `channels.modelByChannel` and `channels.discord.voice.model`

This causes "ghost entries" that point to non-existent providers, visible in Gateway UI and model selection.

## Why This Change Was Made

`cc-switch` is an external Rust binary that directly edits `openclaw.json` without openclaw's mutation helpers. Per CLAUDE.md design:

> "Runtime reads canonical config only. If a config change invalidates existing files, add a matching \`openclaw doctor --fix\` migration."

The robust fix is **defensive self-healing in openclaw** via doctor check, not patching external tools.

## Solution

### Core cleanup function (`pruneOrphanModelRefs`)
- **Deletes** orphan entries from `agents.defaults.models` allowlist map
- **Filters** orphan entries from `fallbacks` arrays
- **Rewrites** orphan primary refs to `agents.defaults.model.primary`
- **Fallbacks** to first available provider if defaults is also orphan
- **Extends** to hooks, messages, and channels config

### Doctor integration
- `openclaw doctor` detects and lists all orphan refs
- `openclaw doctor --fix` automatically repairs config
- Follows existing doctor check patterns (detect + repair)

## User Impact

After this change:
- Users who remove providers via `cc-switch` or manual editing won't see ghost model refs
- `openclaw doctor` warns about orphaned refs with fix hints
- `openclaw doctor --fix` cleans up:
  - ✅ Allowlist map entries (deleted)
  - ✅ Fallback array entries (filtered out)
  - ✅ Primary refs (rewritten to valid provider)
  - ✅ Hooks/messages/channels refs (rewritten)

No runtime performance impact — repair is explicit via doctor command.

## Evidence

- **Unit tests**: 15 passed covering all cleanup scenarios
- **Type check**: `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:dts` passed
- **Test coverage**:
  - Allowlist pruning
  - Fallback filtering
  - Primary ref rewriting with fallback logic
  - agents.list handling
  - hooks/messages/channels cleaning

Fixes the "幽灵条目" (ghost entries) issue.

target.title: chore(ci): harden package-manager resolution
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: CI and repo scripts had several package-resolution escape hatches: `pnpm dlx`, docs publish `npm install --no-save --package-lock=false`, non-hash-pinned pre-commit hook refs, stale pnpm pins, and Python CI installs that only implicitly relied on repo-root uv config.
- Why it matters: These paths are package supply-chain risk multipliers. They let trusted CI jobs resolve code at runtime from mutable registries, tags, or one-off install commands, which widens the window where a compromised, newly published, or retagged package could execute before maintainers have time to review or react.
- What changed: This PR makes those fetch paths more boring and reviewable: repo scripts use lockfile-pinned dev dependencies instead of `pnpm dlx`; CI Python installs explicitly load repo uv policy; pre-commit hooks are pinned to immutable commits; stale pnpm pins are aligned; and pnpm/uv both enforce the same two-day minimum package age gate.
- Regression guardrail: Root `AGENTS.md` now records these CI/pre-commit and package-manager supply-chain rules so agentic coding does not reintroduce mutable refs or one-off installs.
- Lockfile note: The `pnpm-lock.yaml` diff is expected. Replacing `pnpm dlx` with `pnpm exec` means the tools previously fetched on demand now live as dev dependencies, so their transitive dependency graphs are reviewed and pinned in the lockfile. Most of the noise comes from `knip` pulling optional/native `oxc` packages for multiple platforms.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: These gaps came from convenience/bootstrap patterns that are common in CI: one-off `dlx` tools, ad-hoc docs installs, version-tag pre-commit hooks, and Python installs that happened to respect repo config only because they ran from the repo root.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no single guardrail preventing package-manager commands from resolving fresh packages outside the repo lockfile or configured age gate.
- Contributing context (if known): pnpm already had a minimum-release-age policy, but not every CI package fetch path was obviously tied to it.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `Yes`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This PR reduces package-fetch risk by making CI installs prefer lockfile/configured package-manager paths, immutable hook refs, and uv's repo-scoped `exclude-newer = "2 days"` policy. The lockfile changes are intentional: replacing `pnpm dlx` with `pnpm exec` requires adding those tools as dev dependencies so their transitive graphs are reviewed and pinned. Most of the lockfile noise comes from `knip` pulling optional/native `oxc` packages for multiple platforms.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm 10.33.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): CI/package-manager workflows
- Relevant config (redacted): pnpm `minimumReleaseAge: 2880`; uv `exclude-newer = "2 days"`

### Steps

1. `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts=false --config.engine-strict=false --config.enable-pre-post-scripts=true`
2. `pnpm build`
3. `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 .github/workflows/ci.yml pnpm-workspace.yaml pyproject.toml`
4. `pnpm check:changed`

### Expected

- Install and build pass.
- Changed-file formatting passes.
- Package-manager hardening does not introduce build failures.

### Actual

- Install passed.
- Build passed.
- Changed-file formatting passed.
- `pnpm check:changed` passed conflict-marker checks, typecheck, lint, and import-cycle checks; full tests still fail in existing gateway/auth expectation cases unrelated to these package-manager changes.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Relevant local check summary:

```text
[check:changed] summary
   1.19s  ok         conflict markers
  19.42s  ok         typecheck all
  21.29s  ok         lint
   5.75s  ok         runtime import cycles
 206.41s  failed:1   tests all
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: lockfile install, build, changed-file formatting, and changed-check non-test lanes.
- Edge cases checked: uv commands explicitly use `--project "$GITHUB_WORKSPACE"` so future workflow `working-directory` changes do not silently bypass repo uv policy.
- What you did **not** verify: A fully green `pnpm check:changed`, because the full test lane still fails on pre-existing gateway/auth tests outside this diff.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `Yes`
- Migration needed? `No`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: No user migration. CI/package-manager config now consistently applies the two-day package age gate.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The pnpm lockfile diff is large and could look like dependency churn.
  - Mitigation: The large diff is expected from converting `pnpm dlx` tools into lockfile-pinned dev dependencies, especially `knip` and its optional native `oxc` dependency graph.
